headline: The pitfalls of Hungary's post-Orban transition
subhead: Riding a powerful wave of opposition to the outgoing government, Hungary's new prime minister, Péter Magyar, now has to balance the expectations of both left- and right-wing voters while clarifying his positions, especially regarding Ukraine.

headline: Russian strikes kill at least 14 in Ukraine
Concerns mount over private credit in the United States
Pope Leo rebukes Cameroon's elites in the country's capital: 'The chains of corruption must be broken'
subhead: During the second stop of his African tour, the pontiff reminded Cameroon's President Paul Biya of the importance of upholding the rule of law and fundamental freedoms.

headline: 'Our democracies must break their implicit pact with oil'
subhead: A group of academics and political figures, including Lucas Chancel, Thomas Piketty, Jean Pisani-Ferry and Laurence Tubiana, call on public authorities to help the most vulnerable households access electric vehicles.

headline: At Lyon's Musée des Confluences, the Loeb donation celebrates Mali's living arts
subhead: More than 100 masks, puppets and instruments are displayed in a way that evokes the singing, dancing and movements that once accompanied ceremonies along the Niger River.

headline: What is the controversial bill on antisemitism French MPs are debating?
subhead: MP Caroline Yadan has been accused of trying to silence criticism of Israel with her bill, which aims to 'combat new forms of antisemitism.' A petition against the text has gathered over 700,000 signatures.

How Algeria tried to erase the two suicide attacks during Pope Leo's visit
subhead: Two men blew themselves up on Monday about 40 kilometers from Algiers, without causing any casualties other than themselves. Authorities sought to impose silence on the failed terrorist attack from the official narrative of the historic visit.

headline: The Curtis Yarvin conundrum: Should one debate with the far right?
subhead: The reactionary American blogger close to the Trumpian figures was invited to a symposium in Germany with prominent scholars. Some critics say the invitation signals that red lines have vanished from intellectual debate.

headline: New online search tool in Germany reveals if ancestors were Nazi Party members
subhead: In partnership with American and German archives, the weekly 'Die Zeit' has launched a searchable tool granting access to more than 12 million documents, cataloging the bulk of Nazi Party (NSDAP) memberships from 1925 to 1945.

headline: Hungary elections: 'Once the champagne has been popped, European leaders must reassess their expectations for the new government'
